summary: Multi-agent audit of the Iris budget system: math, product gaps, pipeline, UX dead-ends, code health, couples lens
logs: Fanning out 6 dimension reviewers over the Iris codebase

result.summary: The three-lane model itself (budgetLanes.ts) is internally sound — laneOf/isOverBudget/FIXED_OVER_TOLERANCE do what their comments claim, and the insights engine and calculateBudgetSummary apply it correctly. The two systemic problems are: (1) every 'partial month' guard is transaction-count based (>10/>=10/<=10 in different files), which fails at this household's ~180 txns/month — the in-progress month counts as 'full' within days, polluting the savings scorecard's banked/under-base/trend numbers, deflating all bucket averages mid-month, and becoming the Budget overview's default month; and (2) the reserve/work exclusion sets are applied inconsistently across surfaces, so 'Saved This Month', the Cash Flow sub-score, the savings scorecard month strip, and the Dashboard surplus can all disagree about the same month, with the scorecard still painting the April tax payment red. Beyond those, there are several contained but real bugs: the dashboard CashFlowBar double-subtracts investing, refunds are booked as income with the expense-offset branch unreachable, work travel auto-categorized by Teller is invisible to the work-expense trackers, the avg-mode drilldown compares multi-month totals to monthly budgets and annualizes them x12, and UTC date parsing drops 1st-of-month paychecks from month windows.

confirmed.title: In-progress month counted as a 'full month' everywhere (tx-count heuristic fails at real data volume)
confirmed.detail: Every 'skip the partial month' guard is transaction-count based, and at ~180 transactions/month the current month qualifies as 'full' within 2-3 days. (a) Savings scorecard: partial = transactionCount <= 10 || totalIncome === 0 (src/utils/savingsScorecard.ts:93) — by mid-month the current month has >10 txns and a paycheck, so it counts as full: it pollutes cumulativeBanked ('Banked since Sep 2025'), inflates monthsUnderBase (11 days of spend vs the full $15,800 base is almost always 'under'), and sets trend='better' by comparing a half month against May (savingsScorecard.ts:96-107, rendered at src/components/Dashboard/SavingsScorecard.tsx:65-70). (b) Category averages: computeCategoryAverages divides all-months totals by the fullMonths count, and the in-progress month is in BOTH numerator and denominator as if complete (src/utils/transactionAnalysis.ts:110-126 with fullMonths from AppDataContext.tsx:329/460 and BudgetView.tsx:220/292) — so every bucket monthlyActual is understated by up to ~10% early in the month, which overstates budgetSummary.surplus and mutes over-budget flags; conversely months with <=10 txns stay in the numerator but not the denominator. (c) Budget overview default 'latest' resolves to the partial current month (src/components/Budget/BudgetView.tsx:323-335), and Monthly Detail's navigator and 'Surplus / Deficit — Under budget' verdict (BudgetView.tsx:477-497, 561-563) plus YTD averages (BudgetView.tsx:520-521) treat it as complete. Thresholds are also inconsistent: > 10 (AppDataContext.tsx:329, BudgetView.tsx:220/292/1520-1522) vs >= 10 (BudgetView.tsx:323/477, transactionAnalysis.ts:281) vs <= 10 (savingsScorecard.ts:93). Fix is a calendar check (month === current YYYY-MM), not a count.

verdict.note: Confirmed: all cited evidence is real and accurate — savingsScorecard.ts:93 uses `transactionCount <= 10 || totalIncome === 0` (polluting cumulativeBanked/monthsUnderBase/trend at lines 96-107, rendered SavingsScorecard.tsx:65-70); computeCategoryAverages (transactionAnalysis.ts:110-126) puts the in-progress month in both numerator and a `> 10`-count denominator (AppDataContext.tsx:329/460, BudgetView.tsx:220/292); BudgetView 'latest'/Monthly Detail/YTD (323-327, 477-497, 520-521, 561-563) treat the partial month as complete; the >10 / >=10 / <=10 inconsistency is real. No calendar-based exclusion exists anywhere in src — the only partial-month handling is position-based last-month skips in computeMonthComparison (transactionAnalysis.ts:334-335) and computeCategoryTrends (line 237), which makes the claim's "every guard is count-based" a minor overstatement but does not mitigate any cited path. At the documented ~180 txns/month, the current month qualifies as "full" within days.

confirmed.title: Reserve-lane exclusion applied inconsistently — same-page numbers disagree
confirmed.detail: calculateBudgetSummary.realActual and the insights engine exclude all reserve lanes (taxes, travel_personal, travel_work) from operating spend (src/stores/budgetDefaults.ts:369-377; src/utils/insightsEngine.ts:487-489), but BudgetView's totalBucketSpend excludes only travel_work (src/components/Budget/BudgetView.tsx:342, 378). So in a tax-payment month the Budget overview shows 'Over Watermark −$X' (netIncome − totalBucketSpend, BudgetView.tsx:899-908) right next to a positive Cash Flow sub-score built from summary.surplus — and that sub-score's own detail line claims 'take-home − totalBucketSpend' while its number is netTakeHome − realActual, so the displayed arithmetic doesn't reproduce the displayed value (BudgetView.tsx:964-967). The savings scorecard is a third variant: ScorecardMonth.spend = totalExpenses INCLUDING taxes/travel_personal (src/utils/savingsScorecard.ts:85-94), so the $13k April tax payment paints April red/'over base' in the month strip — exactly the false alarm the lane header says it kills (src/utils/budgetLanes.ts:11-14). Pick one definition of operating spend per surface and label which one each number uses.

verdict.note: Confirmed: realActual/surplus exclude all reserve lanes (budgetDefaults.ts:369-377) and insightsEngine.ts:487-489 matches, but BudgetView's totalBucketSpend excludes only travel_work (BudgetView.tsx:378), so the watermark tile (netIncome − totalBucketSpend, BudgetView.tsx:899-908) can show red while the Cash Flow sub-score shows positive surplus — and that sub-score's detail line literally prints "take-home − totalBucketSpend" while its number is take-home − realActual (BudgetView.tsx:964-967), so the displayed arithmetic doesn't reproduce the displayed value. The scorecard's spend = totalExpenses including taxes/travel_personal (savingsScorecard.ts:89-90; transactionAnalysis.ts:88-95 excludes only work), painting tax months red "over base" — contradicting budgetLanes.ts:11-14's stated design that reserve withdrawals are never a budget bust.

confirmed.title: Dashboard CashFlowBar subtracts investing twice
confirmed.detail: calculateBudgetSummary.realActual already includes the investing bucket (it's a FIXED-lane category; the comment at src/stores/budgetDefaults.ts:376-377 says 'investing is already included in realActual via bucket'). DashboardView passes spent={budgetSummary.realActual} AND investing={budgetSummary.investing} to CashFlowBar (src/views/DashboardView.tsx:359-363), which computes surplus = income − spent − investing (DashboardView.tsx:537-543) — investing is deducted twice and also rendered twice (inside the rose 'Spent' segment and as its own violet segment). The card's subtitle uses budgetSummary.surplus (income − realActual), so the header and the bar segments disagree by exactly the investing amount. Currently masked because the Settings investing amount is $0; the moment Scott sets monthly investing, the surplus segment goes wrong silently.

verdict.note: Confirmed: 'investing' is FIXED-lane (budgetLanes.ts:26), so realActual (budgetDefaults.ts:369-371) includes the investing bucket's monthlyActual, which AppDataContext.tsx:307/446 syncs to the Settings investing amount; DashboardView.tsx:359-363 passes both realActual and investing to CashFlowBar, whose line 541 computes income - spent - investing — a double deduction (and double render) the moment Settings investing > 0, while the card subtitle uses budgetSummary.surplus (income - realActual only), disagreeing with the bar by exactly the investing amount. Currently masked only because defaultMonthlyInvestment.amount = 0 (defaultData.ts:236); no compensating exclusion exists on this path (DashboardView.tsx:96 excludes investing only for the top-categories list).

confirmed.title: Refunds are counted as income (and never offset spend) — the outflow-refund branch is dead code
confirmed.detail: classifyBankTransaction emits refunds as { flow: 'inflow', type: 'refund' } (src/utils/transactionCategorize.ts:36-41), but computeMonthlySpending's inflow branch adds every non-reimbursement inflow to totalIncome and continues BEFORE the refund check (src/utils/transactionAnalysis.ts:73-80) — the 'totalExpenses -= amount' refund branch at line 80 is only reachable for outflow-typed refunds, which nothing produces. Net effect: a merchant refund inflates monthly income AND the original charge stays in spend, double-distorting scorecard 'banked', Avg Monthly Income, and Monthly Detail income. Separately, the Teller card import drops card credits entirely as 'card_payment_or_refund' (server/teller-map.ts:134-137), so a refunded card purchase remains as full spend with no offset on any path.

verdict.note: Confirmed. transactionCategorize.ts:36-41 only ever emits refunds as inflow, and transactionAnalysis.ts:73-78 adds every non-reimbursement inflow to totalIncome before the line-80 outflow-refund branch, which no producer can reach (all four CSV refund paths in ExpenseManager.tsx:313/318/338/366 set flow='inflow'; Teller import hardcodes outflow+expense and drops card credits at teller-map.ts:134-138; recategorize.ts:62 forces the outflow path which never returns 'refund'). Refunds therefore inflate totalIncome while the original charge stays in totalExpenses, double-distorting savingsScorecard.ts:88-91 (banked), BudgetView.tsx:1521 (Avg Monthly Income), and Monthly Detail income; incomeDetector's refund handling is a separate feature and does not mitigate this path.

confirmed.title: Two different definitions of 'work expense' — auto-categorized work travel invisible to the trackers
confirmed.detail: computeMonthlySpending excludes (e.isWorkExpense || e.category === 'travel_work') from personal spend (src/utils/transactionAnalysis.ts:88-91) and WorkReimbursementsCard uses the same OR (src/components/Budget/WorkReimbursementsCard.tsx:14-16). But computeWorkExpenses counts only e.isWorkExpense and books unflagged travel_work into 'personal' (transactionAnalysis.ts:161-170), and the Monthly Detail Work Expense Tracker also filters only e.isWorkExpense (src/components/Budget/BudgetView.tsx:700-707). The Teller import always sets isWorkExpense: false while auto-categorizing hotels/airlines to travel_work (server/teller-map.ts:162/188; src/utils/transactionCategorize.ts:153-158, 186-187) — so synced work travel is excluded from monthly spend (correct) but missing from the tracker's 'Total Work Expenses', understating 'Outstanding' against Coupa reimbursements, and the Monthly Detail Work/Personal tiles don't reconcile with the same month's Total Spend. The two reimbursement matchers also differ: BudgetView matches only transactionType === 'reimbursement' (BudgetView.tsx:705) while WorkReimbursementsCard also matches income-source-linked IDs and inflow travel_work (WorkReimbursementsCard.tsx:35-41).

verdict.note: Confirmed: computeMonthlySpending excludes (isWorkExpense || category==='travel_work') (transactionAnalysis.ts:88-91) while computeWorkExpenses (transactionAnalysis.ts:167-168) and BudgetView's Work Expense Tracker (BudgetView.tsx:702-705) filter only isWorkExpense; Teller import hardcodes isWorkExpense:false (teller-map.ts:162/188) while bestCategory auto-assigns travel_work via classifyBankTransaction (transactionCategorize.ts:153-158/186-187), and neither the recategorize endpoint (recategorize.ts:70) nor the sync client ever sets the flag — so synced work travel is invisible to the tracker's totals/Outstanding and the Monthly Detail Work/Personal tiles (BudgetView.tsx:567-568) don't reconcile with Total Spend; the two reimbursement matchers also diverge as claimed (BudgetView.tsx:705 vs WorkReimbursementsCard.tsx:35-41).

confirmed.title: Category drilldown in 'Average' mode compares a multi-month total against a monthly budget and annualizes it x12
confirmed.detail: When the overview is in 'avg' mode, the drilldown's monthFilter returns true for every transaction (src/components/Budget/BudgetView.tsx:1578-1583), so `total` is the category's spend across ALL ~9 imported months. The footer then renders 'Over budget by (total − bucket.monthlyBudget)' against the monthly budget and 'Projected annual spend = total × 12' (BudgetView.tsx:1617-1634) — roughly a 9x overstatement. The drilldown list also includes work-flagged transactions (filter is only category + outflow at BudgetView.tsx:1584-1586), so its total won't match the bucket's monthlyActual, which excludes them.

verdict.note: Confirmed: in 'avg' mode monthFilter returns true for all transactions (BudgetView.tsx:1579) so `total` spans all ~9 imported months, yet the footer compares it to bucket.monthlyBudget and renders total*12 (BudgetView.tsx:1618-1632); the drilldown filter (1584-1586) also includes isWorkExpense transactions that the bucket's monthlyActual excludes (transactionAnalysis.ts:88-92 routes work-flagged spend out of its original category). No compensating normalization exists anywhere in the modal or callers.

unverified.title: UTC vs local date drift drops 1st-of-month transactions from 'This month' / YTD windows
unverified.detail: VariableSurplusCard and WorkReimbursementsCard compare new Date('YYYY-MM-DD') (parsed as UTC midnight) against local-midnight boundaries (new Date(y, m, 1)): in US Central, a paycheck or work expense dated the 1st parses to 6-7pm local on the previous day and is excluded from monthStart/yearStart windows (src/components/Budget/VariableSurplusCard.tsx:117-134; src/components/Budget/WorkReimbursementsCard.tsx:66-77). With semi-monthly paychecks that often land on the 1st, the 'This month' above-base sweep number and the Jan-1 YTD boundary are systematically wrong. Same pattern (lower stakes) in the 7-day new-transactions banner (BudgetView.tsx:428-429). The month-key code elsewhere uses string slicing and is immune — these date-object comparisons are the outliers.

unverified.title: Guaranteed-base derivation counts ALL income deposits in its pay-frequency estimate — $7,900 cliff at 2.5 deposits/mo
unverified.detail: computeGuaranteedBase computes perMonth = Math.round(income.length / distinctMonths) over EVERY transactionType==='income' inflow, then base = modalPaycheck × perMonth (src/utils/savingsScorecard.ts:49-52). The doc comment claims it 'Excludes variable/RSU/OT' but only the modal amount does — the deposit COUNT does not. Any recurring separate-deposit income (commission paid as its own ACH, overlap deposits when Scott changes employers, a one-off typed 'income') pushes the average past 2.5/month and base silently jumps $15,800 → $23,700 (perMonth 2 → 3), flipping every scorecard month's under/over-base verdict. It currently derives correctly only because server/teller-map.ts:210-237 imports income strictly from /ABNORMAL/ deposits — a filter that itself breaks the day the employer changes (Scott is leaving Abnormal). Counting only deposits near the modal amount when estimating pay periods would make this robust.

unverified.title: Dashboard labels multi-month averages as 'this month', and 'Spending this month' sums only the top 6 categories
unverified.detail: dashBuckets monthlyActual values are multi-month averages from applyTransactionsToBuckets (src/context/AppDataContext.tsx:328-332), but the dashboard presents them as current-month numbers: hero 'Cycle · this month' surplus (src/views/DashboardView.tsx:184-193), 'Spending this month' card, and 'Cash flow this month'. Meanwhile the Budget overview defaults to the actual latest month (BudgetView.tsx:325-335) — so Dashboard and Budget show different numbers for what reads as the same concept. Additionally, the 'Spending this month' subtitle is the sum of only the top-6 non-reserve categories (filter→sort→slice(0,6) happens before the reduce, DashboardView.tsx:92-109), and the donut's '% of budget' is top-6 spend over top-6 budgets — both understate the real totals without saying so.

unverified.title: Stash-style categories live in the flexible alarm lane — December gifts will fire the exact false alarm the reserve lane was built to kill
unverified.detail: laneOf() routes gifts_holidays, home_maintenance, and car_maintenance to 'flexible' (not in FIXED_CATEGORIES or RESERVE_CATEGORIES, src/utils/budgetLanes.ts:24-33), yet budgetDefaults documents them as sinking-fund/stash categories ('Sinking fund so December does not wreck you', src/stores/budgetDefaults.ts:339-341) and sampleBudgetBuckets labels them '(Stash)'. A lumpy December gifts spike or a one-off home repair will trigger detectOverBudgetCategories' critical 'over budget by N%' insight (src/utils/insightsEngine.ts:105-137) — the same class of false alarm the reserve lane exists to suppress for taxes/travel. 'debt' (where credit-card interest is categorized, transactionCategorize.ts:217) is also flexible. Relatedly, BudgetView's essentialCats list (BudgetView.tsx:354) omits 'charity' while FIXED_CATEGORIES includes it — the third lane-ish taxonomy in play.

unverified.title: Fresh-user NaN: savings rate divides by grossMonthly with no guard, rendered on the dashboard hero
unverified.detail: calculateBudgetSummary computes savingsRate = (investing + 401k + HSA) / paycheck.grossMonthly * 100 with no zero guard (src/stores/budgetDefaults.ts:378). With the shipped defaultPaycheck (all zeros) this is 0/0 = NaN, and the dashboard hero renders `${budgetSummary.savingsRate.toFixed(0)}% savings rate` whenever surplus >= 0 with no data guard (src/views/DashboardView.tsx:185-192) — a fresh install shows '+$0' and 'NaN% savings rate'. The insights engine guards this case (insightsEngine.ts:217) and BudgetView guards its own copy (BudgetView.tsx:376); only calculateBudgetSummary doesn't.

unverified.title: RESERVE_ALLOCATIONS hardcodes Scott's set-asides and always overrides the user's own reserve budgets
unverified.detail: budgetLanes.ts ships Scott-specific monthly set-asides (taxes: 1500, travel_personal: 1000, travel_work: 0) as code constants (src/utils/budgetLanes.ts:38-42). The Monthly Detail reserve row computes alloc = RESERVE_ALLOCATIONS[c.cat] ?? budget (src/components/Budget/BudgetView.tsx:593) — but since all three reserve keys are defined, the ?? fallback to the user's bucket budget is unreachable: editing the taxes/travel budget in Edit Budget never changes the reserve progress bar or its '$X/mo reserved' copy. Contradicts the neutral-defaults work elsewhere in budgetDefaults.ts and will mislead the moment the real tax reserve changes.

result.summary: Against the Monarch/YNAB/Copilot table-stakes bar, Iris's budget side has genuinely strong bones — transaction search/filtering is near parity, recurring detection is well-engineered, refund/transfer/reimbursement typing exists, a full JSON backup works, and goal math plus an audit-log backend are already written. The systemic weakness is time: there is no real current-month axis (every "this month" surface shows multi-month averages, making pace alerts and the dashboard pulse incoherent), no month-close or budget versioning, and no forward look (forecast). The second theme is unfinished promises: the Settings panel advertises critical alerts with zero implementation and no delivery channel outside the app, the Reserve lane's "it's funded monthly" premise is never actually tracked against a balance, and a complete GoalTracker UI ships in the bundle but renders nowhere. Sharpest quick wins: net refunds per-category (small bug, distorts the exact flexible lanes the new model highlights) and fix the 180-day lookback that makes quarterly/yearly bill detection mathematically impossible.

confirmed.title: No current-month axis — 'this month' surfaces actually show multi-month averages
confirmed.detail: Every surface labeled 'this month' is fed by bucket.monthlyActual, which is a multi-month AVERAGE: applyTransactionsToBuckets divides total spend by the count of full months (src/utils/transactionAnalysis.ts:129-145), and both loaders write that average into the buckets (src/components/Budget/BudgetView.tsx:293, src/context/AppDataContext.tsx:331 and :462). The Dashboard hero 'Cycle · this month' (src/views/DashboardView.tsx:184-193), 'Spending this month' donut (:289), and 'Cash flow this month' bar (:348-365) therefore show the historical average, not month-to-date. Worse, the pace machinery is incoherent on this data: paceTriggers compares averaged monthlyActual against day-of-month calendar fraction ('90% spent on day 10', src/utils/triggerDetector.ts:81-177, fed averaged buckets at BudgetView.tsx:1555), and BudgetPulse draws a calendar-pace tick for 'How the month is going' (src/components/Budget/BudgetPulse.tsx:44-58, 165-169) against buckets that hold either the average or the latest FULL month — which early in a new month is last month's data. Monarch, YNAB, and Copilot all anchor the home screen to the actual in-progress calendar month with month-to-date actuals and 'remaining'. For a household using this daily, this is the single most important gap: the app cannot answer 'how are we doing THIS month, today'.

verdict.note: Confirmed: applyTransactionsToBuckets writes multi-month averages into monthlyActual (transactionAnalysis.ts:122,129-145), both loaders feed it (BudgetView.tsx:293, AppDataContext.tsx:331,462), and all three dashboard "this month" surfaces read those averaged buckets via calculateBudgetSummary(dashBuckets) (AppDataContext.tsx:507; DashboardView.tsx:184-193,289,348-365); paceTriggers compares averaged actuals to day-of-month fraction (triggerDetector.ts:83-85,129-174) fed at BudgetView.tsx:1555, and BudgetPulse draws a calendar tick against average-or-latest-month buckets (BudgetPulse.tsx:44-58,165-169; BudgetView.tsx:323-335). Only nits: "every surface labeled this month" slightly overstates (WorkReimbursementsCard.tsx:80 and VariableSurplusCard.tsx:128 use true month-start windows), and the latest ">10 txn" month can be the in-progress month mid-month — which then also corrupts the average denominator — neither of which refutes the core gap.

confirmed.title: Alerts never leave the app, and the 'Critical — always on' tier is advertised but not implemented
confirmed.detail: The Notification settings panel promises three Critical alerts that 'never go silent' — 'Bill won't clear', 'Paycheck didn't land', 'Suspicious / unusual charge' (src/components/Settings/NotificationSettings.tsx:63-74) — but the trigger engine implements generators for only 3 of the 13 NotificationKeys: pace_80/90/100, surplus_available, income_classification_needed (src/utils/triggerDetector.ts:65-67). bill_wont_clear, paycheck_missing, fraud_suspicious, reimbursement_matched, subscription_confirmed, weekly_summary, monthly_trends, and goal_pace_check (src/types/budget.ts:149-165) have NO code behind them — the settings toggles save preferences nothing reads. Delivery is also in-app only: triggers render solely as cards in TriggerCenter, which mounts only inside the Budget view's Overview tab (BudgetView.tsx:1555) — not even on the Dashboard — so a user who doesn't open Budget never sees an alert. Monarch/YNAB/Copilot all push to phone/email. Acknowledge/Snooze is in-memory useState (src/components/Budget/TriggerCenter.tsx:35,64-67), so dismissals resurrect on every reload. For the deployment model (desktop-hosted, LAN), even browser/desktop notifications or a daily email would close most of this; at minimum the Settings panel should stop advertising alerts that do not exist.

verdict.note: Confirmed: NotificationSettings.tsx:63-74 advertises three always-on Critical alerts whose keys (budget.ts:151-153) have zero generator code — triggerDetector.ts:65-67 implements only pace/surplus/classification, and grep shows the other 8 keys exist only in types + settings toggles nobody reads. TriggerCenter (the sole detectTriggers consumer) mounts only inside BudgetView.tsx:1555 ('overview' section, line 825), dismissals are unpersisted useState (TriggerCenter.tsx:35,64-67), and no Notification API/email/push code exists anywhere in the app. Only nit: "3 of the 13 NotificationKeys" should read 3 generators covering 5 keys.

confirmed.title: Reserve lane and Stashes have no funded-balance tracking; the built GoalTracker UI is orphaned
confirmed.detail: Three disconnected set-aside systems exist and none tracks reality: (1) RESERVE_ALLOCATIONS is hardcoded in source ($1,500/mo taxes, $1,000/mo travel — Scott-specific numbers baked into src/utils/budgetLanes.ts:38-42, at odds with the local-first-distributable target), and the UI claims '$1,500/mo reserved · lumpy, not a monthly bust' (BudgetView.tsx:612) — but nothing anywhere accumulates a reserve balance, so when the $13k April tax bill hits there is no way to know whether 9 months × $1,500 was actually set aside or already spent. (2) SinkingFund.currentBalance is only ever written by a manual number input in edit mode (BudgetView.tsx:1477 is its sole write site) — monthlyContribution never auto-accrues and no transaction links to a fund, so the insight engine's 'falling behind pace' checks (src/utils/insightsEngine.ts:314-370) run on stale hand-typed balances. (3) GoalTracker.tsx (267 lines with on-track/behind/projected-date math) is imported by zero files — a complete goals UI that never renders. YNAB's entire model is funded targets; Monarch links goals to accounts. This matters acutely because the just-shipped three-lane budget excludes reserves from monthly over/under ON THE PREMISE that they're funded — the premise is unverifiable in the app.

verdict.note: Confirmed: RESERVE_ALLOCATIONS is hardcoded Scott-specific at budgetLanes.ts:38-42 and consumed only for display at BudgetView.tsx:593/612 with no balance accumulation anywhere; SinkingFund.currentBalance's only interactive write is the manual edit-mode input at BudgetView.tsx:1476-1477 (no auto-accrual, no Expense→fund linkage; sweep 'sinking_fund' is just a nudge label at triggerDetector.ts:273) while insightsEngine.ts:314-370 computes pace from those hand-typed balances; GoalTracker.tsx has zero importers (orphaned). Minor quibbles only: GoalTracker is 247 lines not 267, and "sole write site" ignores non-accruing paths (backup restore DataBackup.tsx:77, undo snapshot, actionExecutor.ts:535-540).

confirmed.title: Refunds are never categorized and never net against the category they refund
confirmed.detail: Credit-card credits are correctly typed as refunds on import (src/components/Budget/ExpenseManager.tsx:313, :318, :339, :366), but category is only assigned when txType === 'expense' (ExpenseManager.tsx:390), so every refund lands as 'other' with no link to the original purchase's category. computeMonthlySpending then subtracts refunds from the month's totalExpenses but NOT from byCategory (src/utils/transactionAnalysis.ts:80) — so a $300 returned Amazon order permanently inflates that category's actual, the budget bars, the over-budget list, and the pace alerts, while the month total quietly absorbs the credit. Monarch and Copilot net refunds within the category. Fix is small: categorize refunds at import (merchant mapping already exists) and subtract from byCategory in the same branch. For a household with regular Amazon/retail returns this silently distorts exactly the flexible categories the three-lane model says to watch.

verdict.note: Confirmed: refunds are typed at import (ExpenseManager.tsx:313/318/338/366) but category is only assigned when txType==='expense' (ExpenseManager.tsx:390; classifyBankTransaction also hardcodes 'other' for refunds at transactionCategorize.ts:37,40), and computeMonthlySpending subtracts refunds from totalExpenses then `continue`s before the byCategory accumulation (transactionAnalysis.ts:80 vs 95-97) — so per-category actuals feeding budget bars and isOverBudget (via applyMonthToBuckets/applyTransactionsToBuckets → BudgetView.tsx:493, AppDataContext.tsx:331, budgetLanes.ts:68) are gross, never netted, and no other code path handles it. Only nit: the Citi refund line is 338, not 339.

confirmed.title: Quarterly and yearly recurring bills are mathematically undetectable
confirmed.detail: detectRecurring requires minOccurrences=3 within lookbackDays=180 (src/utils/recurringDetector.ts:153-155; RecurringBills passes lookbackDays: 180 explicitly at src/components/Budget/RecurringBills.tsx:66). Three quarterly hits span ~182 days and three yearly hits span ~730 days, so the 'quarterly' (85-100d) and 'yearly' (355-380d) cadence branches (recurringDetector.ts:86-87) and their monthly-load divisors (:260-261) are dead code — annual insurance premiums, registrations, and yearly subscriptions can never surface. Those lumpy annual charges are precisely what the Reserve lane and Stashes exist to pre-fund, and what Monarch/Copilot's subscription detection catches. Small fix: per-cadence lookback (e.g. 400 days for quarterly, 800 for yearly, or accept 2 occurrences with tight amount match for yearly).

verdict.note: Confirmed. The only call site (RecurringBills.tsx:66) passes lookbackDays:180, and recurringDetector.ts:163 drops older expenses, so the max possible interval is 180 days — the 'yearly' branch (recurringDetector.ts:87, requires median ≥355d) and its /12 divisor (:261) are provably unreachable; 'quarterly' (85-100d) is only marginally overstated — it can fire for 85-90-day intervals during a 0-10-day flicker right after the third charge, but never for typical 91-92-day calendar quarters, and budgetLanes.ts handles annual obligations only via hard-coded manual set-asides, not detection. The in-code comment at recurringDetector.ts:143 ("180 catches quarterly, not yearly") even mis-asserts the quarterly case.

unverified.title: No split-transaction support
unverified.detail: The Expense type has no parent/child or split fields (src/types/budget.ts:70-88) and ExpenseManager offers no split action — a transaction has exactly one category and one binary work/personal flag (ExpenseManager.tsx:846-862 makes work-vs-personal all-or-nothing per transaction). Monarch, YNAB, and Copilot all support splits. For this household the pain is concrete: Costco/Target/Amazon runs mixing groceries+kids+electronics (there is literally an 'amazon' catch-all category at types/budget.ts:9 because single-category assignment fails for it), and dinners that are part-work/part-personal can't be partially reimbursable — the work toggle moves the entire amount. Would live in types/budget.ts (splits: {category, amount, isWorkExpense}[] or child rows with parentId), ExpenseManager row UI, and the aggregation loop in transactionAnalysis.ts which would need to fan out splits.

unverified.title: Budget targets aren't versioned, so budget-vs-actual history rewrites itself
unverified.detail: Buckets store a single monthlyBudget number; the Monthly Detail view pairs each PAST month's actuals with TODAY'S budget via applyMonthToBuckets (src/utils/transactionAnalysis.ts:148-158, used at BudgetView.tsx:493 and :334), so every budget edit retroactively changes whether September was 'over by $X' (BudgetView.tsx:654-656 renders that judgment per past month). The 'Auto-suggest' button rewrites all targets in one click (BudgetView.tsx:1100-1127) with no record of what months were judged against before. The audit log does capture budget diffs (auditBudgetEdit, src/stores/auditLogStore.ts:114-134) but nothing replays them into history. Monarch keeps per-month budget values; YNAB is inherently month-versioned. Relatedly there is zero rollover/month-close semantics (grep for rollover/carryover in src: no hits) — when the month flips nothing happens to over/under. Judgment: strict YNAB envelope rollover is optional here (the lane model + 'Saved This Month vs watermark' covers the philosophy), but per-month budget snapshots are needed for the history view to be trustworthy — a small month-keyed budget snapshot table written on month boundary or on edit would fix it.

unverified.title: No transaction undo or edit audit trail; deletes are one silent click
unverified.detail: Budget-target edits get a snapshot/diff/audit flow (BudgetView.tsx:136-172), but transaction edits get nothing: in ExpenseManager, description edits (:799-806), category changes (:826-834), work toggles (:846-856), and deletes (:866-873) all fire saveExpense/deleteExpense immediately with no confirmation, no undo, and no audit entry — AuditAction has no transaction action (src/stores/auditLogStore.ts:7-15). The delete button is a hover-revealed '✕' that hard-deletes a real bank transaction in one click. Compounding it, NO UI anywhere renders the audit log that does exist — getAuditLog/getAuditLogForEntity (auditLogStore.ts:150-160) have zero .tsx callers. For a couples-first app where two adults edit shared data, 'who recategorized this / who deleted that' is table stakes (Monarch shows edit history; YNAB has undo). Smallest useful slice: soft-delete with an undo toast + an audit entry on delete, and a simple audit-log panel in Settings.

unverified.title: No cash-flow forecast (projected balance vs upcoming bills) despite all primitives existing
unverified.detail: Beyond the already-planned Safe-to-Spend number, there is no forward-looking view at all: the only mention of forecasting in the budget code is the '(eventually) cash-flow forecast' comment at src/utils/recurringDetector.ts:9. The primitives are all on disk: recurring candidates carry nextExpectedDate/avgAmount/daysUntilNext (recurringDetector.ts:26-27), IncomeSource carries nextExpectedDate and cadence (src/types/budget.ts:109-116), and Teller balance sync provides real cash balances (src/lib/syncTellerBalances.ts). Monarch and Copilot both show 'projected balance through end of month' / upcoming-bills timelines; this is also the prerequisite for the advertised-but-unbuilt 'Bill won't clear' critical alert. Judgment: build AFTER Safe-to-Spend lands and reuse its plumbing — a simple next-30-days ledger (balance + expected income − confirmed recurring bills) covers the household need. A dedicated bill CALENDAR visualization, by contrast, is skippable: the RecurringBills list already sorts by next-due with overdue flags (src/components/Budget/RecurringBills.tsx:79-84), which is adequate for two adults.

unverified.title: No CSV export of transactions (JSON backup only)
unverified.detail: DataBackup exports a full JSON backup and re-imports it (src/components/Settings/DataBackup.tsx:14-50) — solid for backup/restore — but there is no CSV/spreadsheet export anywhere (no text/csv, toCsv, or .csv download hits in src). Monarch, YNAB, and Copilot all export transactions to CSV. The concrete household uses: handing categorized work-expense or tax-relevant transactions to an accountant, and ad-hoc spreadsheet analysis. Effort is trivial (the data is already loaded in ExpenseManager; serialize filteredExpenses respecting the active filters and reuse the existing Blob-download pattern from DataBackup.tsx:35-41). Low urgency, high convenience-per-line-of-code.

unverified.title: Two independent category filters can silently zero out the transaction list
unverified.detail: ExpenseManager maintains two separate category filter states — categoryFilter (set by clicking tiles in the 'Spending by Category' summary panel, ExpenseManager.tsx:558, :725) and filterCategory (the dropdown in the search bar, :562, :681) — and BOTH are applied conjunctively in the filter (:568 and :573). Pick 'Dining' in the dropdown and 'Groceries' in the panel and the table shows 0 rows with no indication why; the dropdown still shows 'Dining' while the panel highlight shows 'Groceries'. The rest of search/filter is genuinely near competitor parity (text search, month range, flow chips, source chips — :671-774), so this is the one wart: merge the two states into one. (One adjacent miss vs competitors: no amount-range filter, which is handy for 'find that ~$400 charge' — same component if ever wanted.)

result.summary: The bank-data pipeline's happy path is genuinely solid — single-transaction upserts with rollback, deterministic teller_<id> idempotency, batch tags for reversibility, and a thoughtful edit-preserving jsonb merge. The robustness problems cluster at the edges where errors and time pass: Teller failures inside a sync (429s, per-account errors) are collected server-side but never surface to the UI, so partial syncs are recorded as fresh successes and the client's entire rate-limit branch is dead code; the 14-day delta window is anchored to 'now' rather than the last sync, so any lapse over two weeks silently and permanently drops transactions; and pending-transaction lifecycle is entirely unhandled (status never read or persisted, nothing ever deleted), so voided holds become permanent phantom spend. A second tier of drift bugs — re-enrollment leaving stale active connectors, the user's 135 merchant mappings never applying to Teller imports, deleted rows resurrecting inside the sync window, and the hardcoded /ABNORMAL/ employer regex with Scott's job change imminent — will each produce quietly wrong budget numbers within months if not addressed.

confirmed.title: Teller 429s and per-account fetch failures are invisible — sync reports success and resets the staleness clock
confirmed.detail: The import handlers always respond HTTP 200 ok:true and stuff per-connector/per-account Teller failures into an `errors` array (server/api-handlers/teller.ts:419-421, 448-450, 502-515). A TellerApiError 429 thrown by fetchAccounts/fetchAllTransactions therefore never surfaces as an HTTP 429 — the client check `txRes.status === 429` (src/lib/syncTellerTransactions.ts:84) can never be true, making the entire rateLimited branch and the 'Banks are busy' UI state (SyncStatus.tsx:54) dead code. Worse, the client only inspects `errors` for dead tokens — `isDeadToken` matches 401/403/'disconnect' only (syncTellerTransactions.ts:62-65, 90-92) — so a 429, 5xx, or network failure that skips an entire account is silently dropped: the summary is saved, LAST_SYNC_KEY is written (line 112), the UI shows '✓ N new', and the 48h staleness banner is suppressed for two more days while one bank's transactions are simply missing. This is the purest 'silently wrong data' path in the pipeline: a partially rate-limited sync is recorded as a complete, fresh sync.

verdict.note: Confirmed: server/api-handlers/teller.ts catches all per-connector/per-account Teller failures into `errors` and always replies 200 ok:true (lines 419-421, 448-450, 502-515; income variant 572/587/634), and a repo-wide grep shows no server code ever emits HTTP 429 — so the client's `txRes.status === 429` check (src/lib/syncTellerTransactions.ts:84) and the 'Banks are busy' state (SyncStatus.tsx:54) are unreachable dead code. Since isDeadToken (syncTellerTransactions.ts:62-65) only matches 401/403/'disconnect', a 429/5xx/network failure that skips a whole account is silently dropped while LAST_SYNC_KEY is written (line 112), showing "✓ N new" and suppressing the 48h staleness banner despite missing data.

confirmed.title: Delta window is anchored to 'now minus 14 days', not to the last sync — any sync gap over 14 days permanently drops transactions
confirmed.detail: syncTellerTransactions.ts:79 computes `since = isoDaysAgo(DELTA_DAYS)` with DELTA_DAYS=14 (line 20), regardless of when the last successful sync ran. If Scott ignores the amber banner for three weeks (vacation, busy quarter — there is deliberately no auto-sync), the next click pulls only the trailing 14 days: everything between lastSync and day -14 is never imported, with zero signal. The UI has no full-import path to recover — SyncStatus is the only caller of /api/teller/import from src/ (grep confirms; the `since=2025-09-01` default is reachable only by hand-hitting the API). The fix is one line — anchor `since` to min(lastSync - safetyMargin, now-14d) — but as written this is a guaranteed future data hole given the no-auto-sync design.

verdict.note: Confirmed: syncTellerTransactions.ts:79 anchors `since = isoDaysAgo(14)` (DELTA_DAYS at line 20) and uses lastSync only for the 5-min debounce (lines 71-77); the server hard-filters to `t.date >= sinceDate` and stops paginating at the cutoff (server/teller-client.ts:251,255), and grep confirms SyncStatus.tsx:53 is the only UI caller with no full-import/recovery path (ConnectorsPanel only hits /api/connectors/* and balances), so any sync gap >14 days silently and permanently drops the lastSync→now-14d window.

confirmed.title: Pending transactions are imported with no lifecycle handling — voided holds become permanent phantom expenses, and the status isn't even persisted
confirmed.detail: TellerTransaction.status ('posted' | 'pending', server/teller-client.ts:204) is never read anywhere in the import path: classifyTellerTxn (server/teller-map.ts:130-153) keeps any positive card amount, and MappedExpense (teller-map.ts:155-199) doesn't carry a status field, so pendings land in Postgres indistinguishable from posted spend. The pipeline is upsert-only (teller.ts:461-487) and never deletes rows Teller stops returning, so: (a) a canceled/expired pending auth (hotel hold, gas pre-auth, duplicate POS auth) stays in the budget forever as real spend; (b) if Teller assigns a different id when a pending posts — the sync code's own claim that re-pulling 'refreshes pending→cleared changes' (syncTellerTransactions.ts:9) assumes id stability, which is undefended in code — both rows survive and the purchase double-counts. Because status isn't persisted, stale pendings can't even be identified retroactively. Minimum fix: skip status==='pending' at classify time, or persist status and reconcile/expire pendings inside the 14-day re-pull window.

verdict.note: Confirmed: TellerTransaction.status (server/teller-client.ts:204) has zero readers repo-wide; classifyTellerTxn (server/teller-map.ts:136) keeps any positive card amount with no status check; MappedExpense (teller-map.ts:155-172) drops status before persisting; the import is upsert-only (server/api-handlers/teller.ts:461-487) with no deletion/reconciliation of rows Teller stops returning (only manual clears in expenses.ts:116-138, no webhooks), so canceled pending holds persist as phantom spend and cannot be identified retroactively — exactly as claimed, and the src/lib/syncTellerTransactions.ts:9 "refreshes pending→cleared" comment indeed rests on undefended id stability.

confirmed.title: Re-enrolling a bank leaves the old connector 'active' forever — permanent 'needs reconnect' alarm, blocked post-sync refresh, and duplicate-import exposure
confirmed.detail: Every Teller Connect success inserts a brand-new connector row with a fresh crypto.randomUUID() id and status 'active' (src/components/Settings/ConnectorsPanel.tsx:145-166); nothing dedupes by provider_enrollment_id or institution, and no code path ever transitions a connector's status — the sync detects dead tokens (syncTellerTransactions.ts:62-65) but only reports them in the summary, never marking the row. All import handlers iterate every status='active' row (teller.ts:399-405, 555-560). Consequences after the next re-enrollment (which WILL happen — Teller dev-tier enrollments break): (1) the dead old token errors 401 on every sync, so SyncStatus shows '<Bank> needs Scott to reconnect' permanently even though he already did; (2) SyncStatus.tsx:66 skips the post-sync reload whenever brokenBanks is non-empty, so freshly imported data permanently stops appearing without a manual reload; (3) if the old token still works, every account is fetched twice per sync, and if Teller account/transaction ids differ per enrollment, the full 14-day window imports twice under different `teller_<txnId>` ids — there is no secondary (date, amount, account) dedupe anywhere. The 'disconnect' confirm dialog exists (ConnectorsPanel.tsx:207-218) but relies on Scott remembering to delete the old row by hand.

verdict.note: Confirmed: every enrollment inserts a new always-'active' row (ConnectorsPanel.tsx:145-166; connectors.ts upsert keyed on user_id+id only) and no code anywhere transitions connector status (grep: zero matches; 0004_connectors.sql:22 documents 'disconnected' as intended-but-unimplemented), so dead tokens permanently populate brokenBanks (syncTellerTransactions.ts:62-65) which permanently suppresses the post-sync reload (SyncStatus.tsx:66); all import handlers iterate every active row (teller.ts:399-405, 555-560) with only teller_<txnId> idempotency and no secondary date/amount/account dedupe on the Teller path (the only such dedupe, ExpenseManager.tsx:405-427, is CSV-import-only client code).

confirmed.title: Edit-safe upsert has two holes: deleted rows resurrect on every sync, and non-whitelisted fields silently revert
confirmed.detail: (a) Resurrection: deleting a transaction in the UI (ExpenseManager.tsx:867 → budgetStore.deleteExpense → hard DELETE in server/api-handlers/expenses.ts:102-146) leaves no tombstone, so if the row's date is inside the rolling 14-day window the next sync re-inserts it from Teller (teller.ts:465-484). Scott deletes a duplicate or phantom hold, it comes back two days later — and the resurrection arrives with default category from the classifier, not his correction. (b) Whitelist gaps: the ON CONFLICT merge `EXCLUDED.data || jsonb_strip_nulls(jsonb_build_object(...))` (teller.ts:473-481, duplicated at 608-616) preserves exactly seven keys (category, isWorkExpense, reimbursementStatus, notes, recurring, incomeSubtype, incomeSourceId). Any other change to a teller row — description edits, flow/transactionType corrections (e.g. re-typing a misdetected card payment as 'transfer' so it leaves spend), displayName, or fields written by the one-off repair scripts in scripts/ (fix-dubai-rows.ts etc.) — is reverted to the freshly mapped Teller values on the next sync for rows in the window, because handleExpensesSave writes the full data blob (expenses.ts:185-196) but the sync merge only keeps the whitelist. Both failure modes are invisible: no diff is reported, the row just quietly changes back.

verdict.note: Confirmed: (a) delete is a hard DELETE with no tombstone (ExpenseManager.tsx:867 → budgetStore.ts:230 → expenses.ts:137-140) and every sync re-pulls a 14-day window (syncTellerTransactions.ts:20,79) re-inserting deleted rows via INSERT..ON CONFLICT with classifier-default category (teller.ts:464-484, teller-map.ts:186 — merchant mappings are not consulted server-side); (b) the merge whitelist (teller.ts:473-481, duplicated 608-616) keeps only 7 keys while the UI's inline description editor (ExpenseManager.tsx:799-806) and recategorize.ts:70 (flow/transactionType) write non-whitelisted fields via full-blob saves (expenses.ts:193), which silently revert on the next in-window sync. Minor overstatement only: fix-dubai-rows.ts patches category/isWorkExpense/notes, all of which ARE whitelisted and survive.

confirmed.title: /api/expenses/recategorize force-classifies every row as an outflow — corrupts income/inflow rows it touches
confirmed.detail: handleExpensesRecategorize passes `-Math.abs(amt)` for EVERY row to force the outflow merchant-rule path and then writes back `flow` and `transactionType` from that result (server/api-handlers/recategorize.ts:62, 70-71), without ever checking the row's stored data.flow. With ?all=1 (explicitly advertised in the comment at lines 33-35) this is catastrophic: every Teller-imported paycheck ('ABNORMAL SEC-OSV...', category 'income') matches no outflow rule, reclassifies to some expense category or 'other', and is rewritten with flow:'outflow', transactionType:'expense' — 18 imported paychecks (~$188k) would flip into expenses in one POST. Even in default mode (category==='other' only), any inflow stored with category 'other' — which is exactly what the CSV import path produces for inflows, since classifyBankTransaction's inflow branch always returns category 'other' (src/utils/transactionCategorize.ts:20-43) — gets converted to an outflow expense (e.g. an 'AMAZON REFUND' inflow becomes an 'amazon' expense), double-counting spend. The endpoint needs to skip rows whose data.flow === 'inflow' (or classify with the true sign).

verdict.note: Confirmed: server/api-handlers/recategorize.ts:62 force-classifies every row with -Math.abs(amt) and lines 70-71 write back flow/transactionType with no data.flow check, while income rows genuinely live in the same expenses table (handleTellerImportIncome INSERTs MappedIncome with flow:'inflow' into expenses at server/api-handlers/teller.ts:600), so ?all=1 flips every paycheck/reimbursement to outflow/expense, and the CSV import path really does store inflows with category 'other' (ExpenseManager.tsx:307,390; transactionCategorize.ts:20-43 inflow branch always returns 'other'), making default mode convert merchant-matching refunds (e.g. AMAZON REFUND→'amazon') into outflow expenses. No code guard exists anywhere (only a prose warning in NEXT_STEPS.md:18 about a different side effect); minor overstatement only in that default mode skips inflows whose forced classification stays 'other'.

unverified.title: Scott's 135 merchant mappings never apply to Teller-synced transactions — corrections must be redone after every sync
unverified.detail: User corrections in the expense UI persist a MerchantMapping keyed by exact description (ExpenseManager.tsx:210, 832, 854 → actionStore 'merchantMappings' collection — the 135 real mappings migrated 2026-06-10). But those mappings are consulted ONLY in the CSV/TXT file-upload path (ExpenseManager.tsx:245-270, mappingLookup). The Teller import categorizes server-side via the hardcoded classifier exclusively — bestCategory() calls classifyBankTransaction then Teller's slug map (server/teller-map.ts:66-70) and never reads the merchantMappings collection (no server code touches it; grep confirms its only consumers are ExpenseManager and DataBackup). So when Scott fixes a recurring merchant the classifier gets wrong, the fix (i) protects that one row via the upsert whitelist, and (ii) records a mapping that will never fire again for Teller data — next month's transaction from the same merchant arrives miscategorized again. Two categorization brains (hardcoded rules in src/utils/transactionCategorize.ts vs the user's mapping store) drift apart indefinitely; the import path should join against merchantMappings (it's in the same Postgres) before falling back to the classifier.

unverified.title: Income import is hardcoded to /ABNORMAL/ — when Scott changes employers, new paychecks silently stop importing
unverified.detail: classifyTellerInflow keeps a deposit as income only if INCOME_EMPLOYER (/ABNORMAL/i) matches the description; everything else is skipped as 'not_employer' (server/teller-map.ts:210-237). The skip is tallied only in the per-account summary that the sync UI never displays — SyncStatus reads just inserted counts (syncTellerTransactions.ts:96-106). Scott is leaving Abnormal (per the session handoffs, equity/transition work is queued), so within weeks the new employer's payroll will land in checking, be skipped on every sync, and the income side of the budget — guaranteed-base detection, savings scorecard, Net-Take-Home hero — will silently flatline while the UI keeps reporting successful syncs. There is no 'large unmatched deposit' warning to catch it. This isn't a generic hardcoding complaint: it's a dated, known life event colliding with a regex.

unverified.title: Income-import failure is swallowed: sync still marked successful, no UI signal
unverified.detail: In syncTellerTransactions.ts:94-106, /api/teller/import-income is 'best-effort': if the response isn't ok-shaped (server 500 from write_failed, 503 from not_configured, JSON parse failure, network error mid-request would actually throw — but an HTTP-level error response is silently ignored via `if (inc?.ok)`), incomeNew stays 0 and the function proceeds to save LAST_SYNC_KEY and a clean summary (lines 108-114). Expense and income are also two separate HTTP calls / DB transactions, so a half-completed sync (expenses written, income not) is recorded as fully fresh. The 'best-effort' intent is reasonable, but the failure should at least flow into the summary (e.g. an incomeFailed flag) instead of being indistinguishable from 'no new paychecks this window'. Combined with the 14-day window bug, a transient income failure that goes unnoticed past 14 days becomes a permanent income gap.

unverified.title: Each sync fetches every depository account's transactions twice, debounce has a cross-tab race, and pagination truncation is discarded
unverified.detail: Three smaller robustness leaks: (1) Double fetch — handleTellerImport pulls /accounts per connector plus transactions for every account, then handleTellerImportIncome immediately re-pulls /accounts and the same depository transactions for the same window (server/api-handlers/teller.ts:415-452 and 569-589); one sync click costs roughly 2x the Teller requests on a tier the code itself documents as rate-limited (teller-client.ts comment, syncTellerTransactions.ts:4-7), doubling the odds of the silent-429 failure above. (2) Debounce race — the 5-minute debounce is a read-then-write on the Postgres settings key 'last_teller_sync' (syncTellerTransactions.ts:67-77, 112) with no lock; two tabs (or Scott + Claire in partner mode) clicking near-simultaneously both pass the check and both run full syncs. Data stays correct because the upsert is idempotent, but the connectors query in the import path has no ORDER BY (teller.ts:399-405), so two concurrent row-by-row upsert transactions can deadlock — that failure at least surfaces as write_failed. (3) fetchAllTransactions returns a `truncated` flag when maxPages (20×250) is hit, but both import handlers destructure only `{ transactions }` (teller.ts:425, 576) — a truncated pull silently imports a partial window with no warning in the response.

unverified.title: Account→source mapping keyed on hardcoded last_four '1006' and one-card-per-issuer assumptions
unverified.detail: mapAccountSource maps any Citi account to 'credit_card_1', any Capital One to 'credit_card_2', and distinguishes the two BoA checkings by the literal last_four '1006' (server/teller-map.ts:75-88); classifyTellerTxn separately hard-skips last_four '1006' as the transfer-only account (teller-map.ts:142). If BoA ever reissues the account number, the joint account's transfers start importing as spend (or the primary account starts being skipped) with no error. If a second Citi or CapOne card is added, both collapse into the same source — and the Settings 'Clear by source' delete (server/api-handlers/expenses.ts:117-121 deletes by data->>'source') would wipe both cards' history in one click. Fine for today's exact account set, but it fails silently the moment the account set changes, which is the same category of time bomb as the employer regex.

result.summary: The Phase-1 budget surfaces (Dashboard hero, three-lane Monthly Detail, sync UI) are coherent, but the app leaks its parked Phase-2 self everywhere: onboarding, Settings, the setup checklist, and the dashboard's own 'See all in Intelligence' link all route users into views the PHASE_1_LOCK removed from navigation, with copy promising tabs that don't exist. Two outright broken affordances sit in the budget flow itself — TriggerCenter's 'Sweep now'/'See breakdown'/'Classify now' buttons do nothing because BudgetView never passes onAction, and 'Quick Import All' silently drops previously-deselected rows via a stale closure. The biggest dead-end for a spouse is the income story: the app twice says 'set your paycheck in Settings' but no paycheck editor exists, and the Income Sources panel collapses to '0 sources / $0' when detections are dismissed. A cluster of identity/terminology drift (four conflicting names for the same credit card, edit-mode red-flagging the very reserve categories the lane system declares calm, 'needs Scott to reconnect' copy) would make a non-technical partner distrust the numbers.

confirmed.title: TriggerCenter action buttons ('Sweep now', 'See breakdown', 'Classify now') are complete no-ops
confirmed.detail: BudgetView renders <TriggerCenter expenses={expenses} buckets={buckets} /> with NO onAction prop (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\components\Budget\BudgetView.tsx:1555). TriggerCenter.handleAction (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\components\Budget\TriggerCenter.tsx:64-69) only handles kind 'acknowledge'/'snooze' (adds to an in-memory Set) and otherwise just calls onAction?.() — which is undefined. triggerDetector emits buttons with kinds 'sweep_now' ('Sweep now'), 'view_breakdown' ('See breakdown', 'See group breakdown'), and 'navigate' ('Classify now') (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\utils\triggerDetector.ts:121,144,221,260). A spouse clicking 'Sweep now' on a '$X surplus available' alert gets zero feedback and zero effect. Bonus: 'Snooze'/'Acknowledge' state is component-local only (TriggerCenter.tsx:35,66), so dismissed alerts resurrect on every page load — the 30-day snooze implied by SNOOZE semantics never persists.

verdict.note: Confirmed: BudgetView.tsx:1555 is the sole TriggerCenter usage and passes no onAction; TriggerCenter.tsx:64-69 only handles acknowledge/snooze (in-memory Set at line 35, never persisted) and calls undefined onAction for sweep_now/view_breakdown/navigate buttons emitted at triggerDetector.ts:121,144,157,170,221,234,260 — grep finds no other handler for these kinds anywhere in src. Only nit: the "30-day snooze" is the reviewer's inference (triggerDetector.ts:191's 30-day window is a surplus lookback, not a snooze duration), but the non-persistence and dead-button claims are factually exact.

confirmed.title: 'Quick Import All' silently imports only the previously-selected subset (stale closure)
confirmed.detail: ExpenseManager's Quick Import All button does setParsedTransactions(prev => map selected:true) then setTimeout(() => handleSaveImported(), 100) (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\components\Budget\ExpenseManager.tsx:1115). handleSaveImported is a useCallback that closes over parsedTransactions (line 483-484: parsedTransactions.filter(t => t.selected)); the setTimeout invokes the OLD closure from the click-time render, so rows that were unselected at click time (including duplicates the importer auto-deselects, line 1018-1022, which the user is explicitly told to 'Re-select if they're actually new') are never saved. The UI shows every checkbox flipping to checked, then clears the list and jumps to the Transactions tab (lines 517-519), so the user believes everything imported. Rows are dropped with no error and no trace.

verdict.note: Confirmed: ExpenseManager.tsx:1115 schedules setTimeout(() => handleSaveImported(), 100) right after setParsedTransactions; the timer invokes the click-time render's handleSaveImported (useCallback at :483 closing over parsedTransactions at :484), so only rows selected before the click are saved — auto-deselected duplicates (:424) and transfers (:397) are silently dropped while :517-519 clears the queue and navigates away; no ref/guard elsewhere mitigates it.

confirmed.title: Onboarding wizard sells and routes into Phase-2 views that PHASE_1_LOCK then hides
confirmed.detail: The wizard's module step offers Investments/Equity/Wealth (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\views\OnboardingView.tsx:45-55) and, if Investments is picked, runs the Risk + 'Load your portfolio' steps; the CSV and Manual import options do saveSetting('onboarding_complete') + setView('portfolio') (OnboardingView.tsx:1088,1094) — dropping the user into PortfolioView, a view the sidebar cannot show because PHASE_1_LOCK force-disables all modules (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\hooks\useEnabledModules.ts:32,46-50). The step copy even says 'upload on the Investments tab' (1087) — a tab that will not exist. The Done step tip tells every new user to 'Try the Intelligence → Market tab... or Ask Iris' (OnboardingView.tsx:1125) — both hidden in Phase 1. A spouse finishing setup is told to use three surfaces that are not in the app she sees. The wizard never checks PHASE_1_LOCK.

verdict.note: Confirmed: OnboardingView.tsx:606 renders MODULE_OPTIONS (lines 45-55) with no Phase-1 filter, lines 1088/1094 do saveSetting('onboarding_complete')+setView('portfolio'), and line 1125's tip references Intelligence/Ask Iris — while useEnabledModules.ts:32 (PHASE_1_LOCK=true, force-disable at 46-50) makes AppShell.tsx:52/204 hide portfolio/intelligence/chat from all navigation; grep shows PHASE_1_LOCK is referenced nowhere else, and App.tsx:35 renders PortfolioView unguarded, so the wizard genuinely drops users into unreachable surfaces (App.tsx:62-66 even added this exact guard for the analogous first-report auto-route, but onboarding lacks it).

confirmed.title: Dashboard 'See all N in Intelligence →' leads to a hidden view that does not contain those insights
confirmed.detail: When insights.length > 5, the 'Iris noticed' panel renders 'See all {N} in Intelligence →' → setView('intelligence') (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\views\DashboardView.tsx:277-281). Two problems: (1) Intelligence is locked out of the sidebar in Phase 1 (useEnabledModules.ts:32, visibleViews), so the user lands in a view with no nav highlight and no way to know where they are; (2) the insights are budget insights from insightsEngine (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\context\AppDataContext.tsx:28,128) while IntelligenceView renders portfolio signals from generateIntelligenceReport and never reads insights at all (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\views\IntelligenceView.tsx:59,153-156) — so the promised 'all N' list literally is not there. Since the dashboard only shows insights.slice(0,5) (DashboardView.tsx:263), insights 6+ are unreachable anywhere in the app.

verdict.note: Confirmed: DashboardView.tsx:277-281 sends users to 'intelligence' which PHASE_1_LOCK (useEnabledModules.ts:32) hides from the sidebar (AppShell.tsx:27 filters via visibleViews; App.tsx:38 renders it anyway with no guard), and IntelligenceView.tsx:59,153-156 never reads context insights — it renders generateIntelligenceReport portfolio signals, while the budget insights (AppDataContext.tsx:28,128) are only ever rendered as insights.slice(0,5) on the dashboard (DashboardView.tsx:263), leaving insights 6+ with no UI surface anywhere.

confirmed.title: No paycheck editor exists anywhere, but two surfaces tell the user to 'set it in Settings'
confirmed.detail: Budget Health's empty state says 'Set your paycheck in Settings' with CTA 'Set income' → settings (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\components\Budget\BudgetView.tsx:920-927), and the auto-derive comment promises 'User can override in Settings' (BudgetView.tsx:304). unverified.title: Income Sources panel shows '0 sources' + $0/$0/$0 tiles when all sources are dismissed, and headline totals change when 'Show dismissed' is toggled
unverified.detail: When every detected source is dismissed, merged.length > 0 so the helpful 'No inflows detected yet' empty state is skipped (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\components\Budget\IncomeSources.tsx:270-285), but visible filters out dismissed (lines 225-231), so the panel renders header badge '0 sources' and Budgetable/Variable/Total tiles all at $0 (lines 296, 320-342) with an empty list — it reads as data loss, with only a tiny '+ Show N dismissed' footer link as a clue. Worse, the headline math is computed from `visible` (lines 236-241), and `visible` INCLUDES dismissed sources when the display toggle is on — monthlyBudgetableIncome only checks includeInBudget, not status (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\utils\incomeDetector.ts:521-528) — so clicking 'Show dismissed' inflates 'Budgetable / mo' and 'Total / mo'. A display toggle should never change money totals. Should instead show 'N sources dismissed — restore?' as the primary state.

unverified.title: Edit Budget mode contradicts the shipped three-lane system: reserve categories show strict red 'over' styling and Taxes sits under 'Discretionary / Lifestyle'
unverified.detail: The new lane system (budgetLanes.ts) says reserves (taxes, travel) are NEVER over and fixed bills get a 15% tolerance. But Edit-mode category rows compute `over = b.monthlyActual > b.monthlyBudget` strictly (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\components\Budget\BudgetView.tsx:1177,1241) and paint red borders/backgrounds (1189-1190,1253-1254), so a lumpy $13k April tax payment that the dashboard and Monthly Detail deliberately treat as calm shows as screaming red the moment the user clicks 'Edit Budget'. Grouping also disagrees: edit mode's essentialCats (BudgetView.tsx:354) omits 'charity' (FIXED lane per budgetLanes.ts:24-27) and has no reserve group, so Taxes, Travel, and Charity all land under 'Discretionary / Lifestyle' (BudgetView.tsx:1238-1240) while the Monthly Detail tab files them under 'Fixed & On Target' / 'Reserves' (BudgetView.tsx:675-679). Same categories, opposite verdicts, one click apart.

unverified.detail: NudgeCenter (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\components\Dashboard\NudgeCenter.tsx), SynthesisDigestCard (Dashboard\SynthesisDigest.tsx), GoalTracker (Dashboard\GoalTracker.tsx), and ProgressTracker (Progress\ProgressTracker.tsx) are never imported by any view (grep across src/*.tsx finds no importer); NudgeCard is only used by the dead NudgeCenter. unverified.title: Dashboard 'Spending this month' card shows only the top-6 categories' total but labels it as total spending and '% of budget'
unverified.detail: spendingByCategory is sliced to 6 before totals are computed (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\views\DashboardView.tsx:98), then totalSpending/totalBudget sum only those 6 (lines 107-108) and feed both the card subtitle 'Spending this month: $X' (line 289) and the donut's '{N}% of budget' center label (lines 293-296). unverified.title: Budget 'Action Items' tab renders a completely blank screen when there are no items
unverified.detail: BudgetView's Action Items tab renders ActionItemsView directly (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\components\Budget\BudgetView.tsx:470-472).
