unverified.title: Single-user hardcodes leak into shared UI copy: 'needs Scott to reconnect', 'manageable in TX', Scott-keyed fun-money emoji, 'fun_wife' category
unverified.detail: SyncStatus renders broken-bank errors as '<Bank> needs Scott to reconnect' for whoever is logged in (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\components\Dashboard\SyncStatus.tsx:60-62) — Claire syncing sees her own app tell her to get Scott. Budget Health's housing advice hardcodes 'Slightly high but manageable in TX' (BudgetView.tsx:961) regardless of profile.state. Fun Money picks emoji via `fm.person === 'Scott' ? '🎮' : '💅'` (BudgetView.tsx:1415) and the category IDs are literally fun_scott / fun_wife (budgetDefaults.ts:335-336) behind 'Personal Fun (1)/(2)' labels. Each is small, but together they undermine the couples-first positioning the moment the spouse reads any of them — and they're all in generic, always-rendered components.

unverified.title: Minor terminology and iconography drift: sidebar logo 'S' for an app named Iris; 'Surplus / Deficit' tile says 'Under/Over budget'; category emoji map misses 9 categories
unverified.detail: The sidebar and mobile header brand mark is the letter 'S' (Signal legacy) next to the name 'Iris' (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\components\Layout\AppShell.tsx:70,92) while onboarding uses an 'i' mark (OnboardingView.tsx:398-399). Monthly Detail's 'Surplus / Deficit' tile computes income − spend but captions it 'Under budget'/'Over budget' (BudgetView.tsx:561-563) — a month can be under budget yet in deficit, so the caption lies about what the number is. txDisplay.categoryEmoji keys 'health' (not 'healthcare', the real id used by the classifier and buckets) and omits childcare, taxes, charity, clothing, gifts_holidays, home_maintenance, car_maintenance, personal, electronics (C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\utils\txDisplay.ts:78-87), so those transactions render the generic 🧾 receipt in Dashboard recent-activity and Spend-by-account, while the same categories show proper icons inside BudgetView.

result.summary: The codebase is in better shape than its size suggests — the lane math, scorecard, categorizer, and transaction aggregation are already pure, exported functions, so the redesign can be de-risked cheaply. The real hazards are structural: the budget data pipeline is pasted 4-5 times across AppDataContext and BudgetView, the un-memoized context (with chat input inside it) re-renders all 20 consumers per keystroke, and BudgetView persists derived bucket actuals back to Postgres on every page view (the documented clobber mechanism), with saves inside setState updaters and un-debounced per-keystroke full-collection writes. Ordered extract-don't-rewrite plan by risk-reduction per hour: (1) install Vitest + ~55 tests on the 7 already-pure modules (~half day, zero code moves); (2) delete dead code — 7 orphan files ~1,150 lines, the waterfall machinery, the no-op migrations (~2h); (3) canonicalize the duplicated helpers — one formatCurrency, one monthKey, one categoryMeta map merged with budgetLanes so 'essential' and 'lane' stop disagreeing about charity/investing (~3h); (4) fix the state hazards — loadBudgetSnapshot() shared loader, memoized/split context, stop write-on-read (~5h); (5) decompose BudgetView along the 10 named seams, then AppDataContext along its 5 (~1-2 sessions); (6) extract the ExpenseManager bank parsers with fixtures. Steps 1-4 are the actual gate for the UI redesign; step 5 can overlap with it.

confirmed.title: Category metadata exists in 3 conflicting maps, and the essential/fixed taxonomy exists in 3 conflicting lists
confirmed.detail: Label/icon maps: transactionAnalysis.ts:174 CATEGORY_INFO, ExpenseManager.tsx:8 DEFAULT_CATEGORY_OPTIONS, txDisplay.ts:78 categoryEmoji. They disagree: categoryEmoji maps 'health' but the real category id is 'healthcare', so dashboard Recent Activity (DashboardView.tsx:381) falls back to the generic receipt emoji for all healthcare spend; travel_work is ✈️ in txDisplay vs 💼 elsewhere; subscriptions 📺 vs 📱; entertainment 🎬 vs 🎟️. txDisplay.ts:9-12 also documents two MORE conflicting account-label maps in ExpenseManager/SettingsView. Worse for the redesign: the essential/fixed taxonomy is triple-defined and inconsistent — essentialCats (BudgetView.tsx:354, includes investing, excludes charity), essLock (BudgetView.tsx:1102 inside Auto-suggest, excludes investing AND charity), FIXED_CATEGORIES (budgetLanes.ts:24, includes charity AND investing). The edit table groups by isEssential while Monthly Detail groups by laneOf, so charity appears as a Fixed bill on one screen and Discretionary/Lifestyle on another. A redesign should consume ONE categoryMeta module (id, label, icon, lane) merged into/next to budgetLanes.ts.

verdict.note: Confirmed in full: txDisplay.ts:84 maps 'health' while the real id is 'healthcare' (types/budget.ts:4), so DashboardView.tsx:381 falls back to 🧾 for healthcare spend; icon conflicts (travel_work ✈️/💼, subscriptions 📺/📱, entertainment 🎬/🎟️) verified across txDisplay.ts:80-86, transactionAnalysis.ts:174-206, ExpenseManager.tsx:8-35; taxonomy is triple-defined and inconsistent (BudgetView.tsx:354 essentialCats has investing/no charity, BudgetView.tsx:1102 essLock has neither, budgetLanes.ts:24 FIXED_CATEGORIES has both), and charity shows as Fixed in Monthly Detail (laneOf grouping, BudgetView.tsx:682) but Discretionary/Lifestyle in the edit table (isEssential grouping, BudgetView.tsx:1240); no unified categoryMeta module exists.

confirmed.title: Budget data pipeline pasted 4-5 times: AppDataContext mount vs view-switch effect vs BudgetView mount vs loadExpenses
confirmed.detail: The sequence load-buckets → syncInvestingBucket → filter real expenses → computeMonthlySpending → fullMonths(count>10) → applyTransactionsToBuckets → fun-money auto-compute → generateInsights exists nearly verbatim in AppDataContext.tsx:304-360 (mount) and 432-491 (every dashboard visit, deps [view] with eslint-disable), and again in BudgetView.tsx:238-320 (mount) and 210-231 (loadExpenses callback). syncInvestingBucket alone is defined twice in AppDataContext (306-307, 445-446) and inlined 3x in BudgetView (226, 259, 295). The real-expense predicate `(e.flow || 'outflow') === 'outflow' && (e.transactionType || 'expense') === 'expense'` appears 24 times across 12 files. Extraction: a loadBudgetSnapshot() function plus exported isRealExpense()/isRealIncome() predicates in transactionAnalysis.ts. This is the single highest-leverage extraction because every categorization or lane change currently needs 4+ synchronized edits.

verdict.note: Core claim verified line-by-line: the pipeline is pasted in AppDataContext.tsx:304-360 and 432-491 (deps [view] + eslint-disable at 491, syncInvestingBucket duplicated at 306-307/445-446) and in BudgetView.tsx:238-320 and 210-231 with the investing-sync map inlined at exactly 226/259/295, and no isRealExpense/loadBudgetSnapshot helper exists anywhere (transactionAnalysis.ts itself inlines the predicate at 163-164 and 275-276). One stat is inflated: the exact predicate appears 8 times across 4 files (~16-18 across 8 counting partial variants), not 24 across 12 — but that doesn't refute the duplication or the extraction recommendation.

confirmed.title: Context value never memoized + chat input lives in global context: every chat keystroke re-renders all 20 consumers
confirmed.detail: AppDataContext.tsx:719-735 rebuilds the value object on every provider render with no useMemo, and 20 files call useAppData(). Because chatInput/setChatInput are IN this context (lines 46-47, 116), each keystroke in the chat box re-renders every subscribed view (Dashboard donuts, recharts areas, etc.). Derived values computed un-memoized on every render (493-510: totalLiquid, allocations, healthMetrics, calculateBudgetSummary, budgetOverBudget) amplify the cost. A UI redesign adds consumers and multiplies this. Minimum fix before redesign: move chatInput local to ChatView, wrap value in useMemo; better: split into BudgetDataContext / PortfolioDataContext / ChatContext along the seams already visible in the value object. Also rawExpenses is typed any[] (lines 58, 126) — type it Expense[] while splitting.

verdict.note: Confirmed: AppDataContext.tsx:719-735 builds the context value inline with no useMemo; chatInput/setChatInput are in the context (lines 46-47, 116, 721) and ChatView.tsx:102 binds the textarea to them, so each keystroke re-renders the provider, re-runs un-memoized derived computations (lines 493-510), and re-renders all mounted consumers (20 files call useAppData; AppInner/AppContent in App.tsx:31,58 are always mounted so the whole shell cascades); rawExpenses is any[] at lines 58/126; no React.memo or context-selector mitigation exists anywhere. Minor caveat: the view router (App.tsx:32-44) unmounts inactive views, so Dashboard charts don't literally re-render while typing today — but they would the moment a redesign mounts them persistently, which is the claim's stated risk.

confirmed.title: BudgetView write-on-read: viewing the budget persists derived data back to Postgres, with saves inside setState updaters and per-keystroke full-collection writes
confirmed.detail: Three compounding hazards. (1) BudgetView.tsx:223-229 calls saveBudgetBuckets(updated) INSIDE the setBuckets updater function — side effects in updaters run twice under React 18 StrictMode and are fire-and-forget; lines 293-297 repeat the pattern on mount. (2) The derived monthlyActual (a pure function of expenses) is persisted to the DB on every page view — this write-on-read is exactly the clobber mechanism budgetLanes.ts:16-18 documents as the reason lane classification cannot live in bucket data ('the running app rewrites the buckets collection'). (3) Every keystroke in an edit-mode number input fires an unawaited whole-collection replace (updateBucket at 1180-1184 and 1244-1248 → saveBudgetBuckets → saveCollection full upsert, budgetStore.ts:90-92) with no debounce — two fast keystrokes can land out of order and resurrect a stale array. For the redesign: compute monthlyActual at render time (it already is, via applyTransactionsToBuckets), persist ONLY user-edited monthlyBudget values, debounce saves, never save inside updaters.

verdict.note: Confirmed: BudgetView.tsx:223-229 calls saveBudgetBuckets unawaited inside the setBuckets updater (StrictMode on at main.tsx:61), the mount effect (287-297) persists derived monthlyActual to Postgres on every view (matching the clobber warning at budgetLanes.ts:16-18), and updateBucket (1180-1184, 1244-1248) fires an undebounced full-collection upsert (budgetStore.ts:90-92) per keystroke from onChange handlers; only nit is that lines 293-297 await the save outside the updater, repeating write-on-read rather than the inside-updater pattern.

confirmed.title: BudgetView decomposition map: 10 named seams that take it from 1,643 lines to a ~250-line shell
confirmed.detail: Concrete seams with line ranges: (a) computeBudgetDiffs 31-104 → utils/budgetDiff.ts (already pure, top test target); (b) edit-mode machine 124-208 + addBucket 174-197 → useBudgetEditMode hook (snapshot/dirty/save/cancel — note dirty-check at 200-208 JSON.stringifys three arrays per keystroke); (c) load pipeline 210-320 → useBudgetData hook shared with the loadExpenses dup; (d) overview derivations 322-378 (fullMonths, overviewBuckets, health sub-scores 358-363) → utils/budgetOverview.ts pure selectors; (e) Monthly Detail section 475-822 → MonthlyDetail.tsx, with the lane-rendered rows 583-696 as LaneCategoryRow; (f) Work Expense tracker 699-782 → component (overlaps the existing WorkReimbursementsCard — consider merging); (g) edit-mode table 1080-1393 → BudgetEditorTable.tsx, where the Essentials rows 1176-1234 and Discretionary rows 1240-1298 are byte-identical 60-line copies (including two identical updateBucket closures at 1180/1244) → one EditableBucketRow; (h) Auto-suggest algorithm inlined in an onClick 1100-1127 → utils/budgetAutoSuggest.ts (pure, testable); (i) FunMoney/Stashes editors 1401-1496 → cards; (j) drilldown modal 1575-1640 → CategoryDrilldownModal.tsx. Dead bits to delete during the pass: write-only categoryTrends state (line 122, computed twice at 218/291 and discarded), empty fragment 1397-1398, unused geminiAvailable prop (466 → ExpenseManager.tsx:146).

verdict.note: Confirmed: BudgetView.tsx is exactly 1,643 lines and all ten seam ranges, the loadExpenses duplication (216-218 vs 289-291), and all three dead bits (write-only categoryTrends at 122/218/291, empty fragment 1397-1398, unused geminiAvailable at 466 → ExpenseManager.tsx:142/146 whose own comment says "unused for now") check out, and none of the proposed extraction files exist anywhere in src. Only nit: the two 59-line row blocks are not literally byte-identical — diff shows 2 differing lines (filter predicate at 1176/1240 and annualized threshold 10000 vs 5000 at 1196/1260) — though the updateBucket closures at 1180/1244 are character-identical as claimed.

confirmed.title: AppDataContext decomposition map: 5 named seams
confirmed.detail: (a) Portfolio migrations block 146-269 → delete or scripts/migrations module (see separate finding); (b) data-load pipeline 271-427 → loadAppData() + loadBudgetSnapshot() in src/lib, leaving the effect as a thin caller — this also unifies it with the duplicated view-switch effect 432-491; (c) chat machinery 567-667 (sendMessage routing, image upload) → useChat hook or ChatContext — it is the only part that needs chatMessages/chatInput; (d) price refresh + snapshot writing 682-717 and 368-397 → usePriceRefresh / snapshotService (note the snapshot-building code at 368-395 and 692-705 is itself duplicated); (e) derived selectors 493-565 → pure functions in utils (totalNetWorth assembly at 493-502 is repeated at 693-698). After extraction the provider is ~150 lines of state + wiring, which is what a redesign can safely re-skin against.

verdict.note: Confirmed: all five seams exist at the cited lines in C:\Claude\projects\signal\signal-app\src\context\AppDataContext.tsx — migrations end exactly at 269, load pipeline 271-427 duplicates the view-switch effect 432-491, chat machinery 567-667, price refresh 682-717 duplicates snapshot/net-worth assembly 368-397 (formula at 493-502 repeats at 693-698 and 370-375); no loadAppData/useChat/usePriceRefresh/snapshotService exists anywhere in src, so nothing handles this already. Only nit: seam (a)'s start (146) includes ~12 lines of account-loading setup before the first migration at ~158-162, and "only part that needs chatMessages" slightly overstates (history load at 287-288, scroll effect at 429) — neither undermines the finding.

unverified.title: Two diverging formatCurrency/formatPercent implementations are both in live use
unverified.detail: src/utils/format.ts:2 renders >=$1M as '$1.2M' (1 decimal); src/utils/calculations.ts:214 renders '$1.23M' (2 decimals). AppDataContext re-exports the calculations version (src/context/AppDataContext.tsx:13,33), so DashboardView (src/views/DashboardView.tsx:6) formats the net-worth hero with one formatter while BudgetView imports utils/format (src/components/Budget/BudgetView.tsx:25) — at the current ~$550k trending toward $1M, the same number will render differently per screen. formatPercent also diverges: format.ts:7 (unsigned, 1dp) vs calculations.ts:225 (signed '+x.xx%', 2dp). Two more local copies: ExpenseManager.tsx:37 (cents version — keep, it is intentional for transactions, but name it formatCents) and dead GoalTracker.tsx:11. Fix: keep utils/format.ts as canonical, have calculations.ts re-export it, fix AppDataContext line 13 import source.

unverified.title: Month-key date parsing re-implemented 8 times; the canonical exported helper already exists but is barely used
unverified.detail: src/utils/txDisplay.ts:59 exports monthKey() handling both M/D/YYYY and ISO, yet the identical logic is privately re-implemented in src/utils/transactionAnalysis.ts:31 (getMonthKey), src/utils/savingsScorecard.ts:16, inlined 3x in BudgetView.tsx (712, 716, 1581) and 2x in AppDataContext.tsx (519-524, 553-559). Month-LABEL formatting is similarly duplicated: txDisplay.ts:70 monthLabel() vs transactionAnalysis.ts:40 getMonthLabel() vs three inline toLocaleDateString copies in BudgetView (842-845, 1001, 1091). txDisplay.ts:58 even has a comment saying it 'mirrors the inline logic in AppDataContext'. One import swap per site; behavior already identical, so this is pure consolidation.

unverified.title: Dead waterfall machinery still computing in AppDataContext (~60 lines + 5 context fields, zero consumers)
unverified.detail: The Paycheck Waterfall UI was removed 2026-06-11, but its engine survives: waterfallMonth state (AppDataContext.tsx:127), availableMonths memo (513-529), activeWaterfallMonth (532-534), and the waterfallBuckets memo (537-565) which re-runs computeMonthlySpending + applyTransactionsToBuckets over all 1,688 transactions every time rawExpenses or dashBuckets changes. Grep across src/ finds zero consumers of waterfallBuckets/activeWaterfallMonth/setWaterfallMonth/availableMonths outside the context itself (only a stale comment at txDisplay.ts:58). Delete the state, both memos, and the 5 fields from AppDataContextValue (60, 82-84) and the value object (725, 731).

unverified.title: Seven dead files (~1,150 lines): both lib migration modules, actionExecutor.verify, depositAdvisor, and 3 dashboard components
unverified.detail: Zero importers (verified against static and dynamic imports): src/lib/migrate-browser-stores.ts (77L), src/lib/migrate-indexeddb-to-postgres.ts (461L — the IndexedDB era is over per the 2026-06-10 de-browserification), src/utils/actionExecutor.verify.ts (243L dev harness sitting in the prod tree), src/utils/depositAdvisor.ts (no references at all), src/components/Dashboard/GoalTracker.tsx (also hosts a formatCurrency clone), src/components/Dashboard/NudgeCenter.tsx, src/components/Dashboard/SynthesisDigest.tsx. Deleting these shrinks the surface the redesign has to reskin and kills a chunk of the 145 lint findings for free.

unverified.title: ~90 lines of one-shot portfolio migrations run on every app launch; two blocks are provably no-ops
unverified.detail: AppDataContext.tsx:160-244 embeds five data migrations in the mount effect. The wife-401k placeholder fix (163-170) and 401k-notes propagation (172-191) both require defaultAccounts.find(...) to match — but defaultAccounts is [] (src/stores/defaultData.ts:232), so they are dead by construction. The SHIB/DOGE→SOL consolidation (192-201), the hardcoded personal crypto share counts 3.16751387 BTC / 57.440110974 SOL (206-231), and SOXX→SOXQ (233-244) are one-time Apr-2026 fixes still executing per launch. Riskiest part: migrationChangedValues triggers clearSnapshots() (364-366), and the always-on 'data integrity' recalc (246-269) also sets it — a rounding drift can silently wipe the entire net-worth history chart. Delete the no-ops, move the rest behind a versioned runMigrations(accts) module (or delete outright since they already ran on the only real dataset).

unverified.title: Minimal test scaffold: ~55 unit tests over 7 already-pure modules, zero refactoring required first
unverified.detail: Every high-risk calculation is already a pure function, so tests can pin behavior BEFORE any code moves: (1) budgetLanes.ts laneOf/isOverBudget — ~8 cases incl. the 1.15x fixed-tolerance boundary and budget<=0; (2) savingsScorecard.ts computeGuaranteedBase (modal-paycheck rounding to $50, per-month multiplier) + computeScorecard (partial-month heuristic `transactionCount <= 10 || totalIncome === 0` at line 93, since-cutoff, trend) — ~10 cases; (3) transactionAnalysis.ts computeMonthlySpending routing — ~12 cases for the load-bearing branches: work-flag-wins-over-category (line 88), refund subtraction (80), reimbursement excluded from income (76), transfer/investment short-circuits (71-72); (4) transactionCategorize.ts classifyBankTransaction/guessCategory — snapshot over a fixture of ~50 real-shaped merchant strings (EXXON→transportation regression); (5) budgetDefaults.ts calculateBudgetSummary (line 363) — reserve-lane exclusion + the unguarded division producing NaN savingsRate when grossMonthly=0, which DashboardView.tsx:190 renders as 'NaN% savings rate' for a fresh user (`budgetSummary.savingsRate.toFixed(0)`); (6) txDisplay.ts monthKey/monthLabel — 5 format cases; (7) BudgetView's computeBudgetDiffs (31-104) — ~6 add/edit/remove cases (import from the component file now, move later). One Vitest install + ~55 tests ≈ half a day, and it is the cheapest insurance for both the decomposition and the redesign.

unverified.title: ExpenseManager (1,238L) carries the bank-file parsers inline — pure-function extraction with fixture tests
unverified.detail: detectFileFormat (ExpenseManager.tsx:59-82), sourceFromFormat (84-96), parseBofATxt (100-137), and the per-format CSV parsing inside handleFileUpload (241-481) are pure string→transaction functions embedded in a component. The format-detection ordering is fragile by its own comments ('Must come BEFORE broad Citi fallback', line 73) — exactly the kind of logic that silently breaks on edit. Extract to src/utils/bankParsers/ and snapshot-test against one fixture file per format (bofa_txt, bofa_cc, chase_cc, citi_cc, capital_one_cc, generic_csv). Lower urgency now that Teller is the primary ingest path, but it is the third-largest file and the parsers are the only part with real logic risk.

result.summary: The couples-first bones are genuinely there — onboarding captures a partner with her own PIN (OnboardingView.tsx), the dashboard greets 'Scott & Claire', HouseholdEarners collects per-earner profiles, the schema carries user_id everywhere, and fun-money/per-person insights exist in the engine — but today multi-user is cosmetic: AppDataContext discards activeUser (line 104), the only per-user state is a tutorial flag, the audit log records no actor, and every screen Claire sees is Scott's screen. The sharpest fault line is Scott-creep that survived the claimed cleanup: literal 'Scott'/'Claire' string matching silently breaks fun-money spend tracking for any other names, the category enum is fun_scott/fun_wife, and the reserve plan is Scott's personal numbers hardcoded in budgetLanes.ts. The minimum set that turns this into a shared daily ritual is small and mostly assembly, not construction: thread activeUser into audit entries, de-hardcode fun-money attribution via Earner profiles, surface the (currently edit-mode-buried) per-person Fun Money meters on the Dashboard, and mount the already-built-but-orphaned GoalTracker for shared goals — plus the half-day OTE/RSU/base-sweep vocabulary pass already spec'd in the backlog. None of this requires the parked Phase 2 co-op mechanics; it converts the existing dashboard from Scott's solo HUD into a glanceable two-player scoreboard.

confirmed.title: Multi-user login is cosmetic — activeUser is accepted and immediately discarded
confirmed.detail: Login/user-switching today changes nothing but the sidebar avatar. LockScreen lets a household pick a user + PIN (src/components/Auth/LockScreen.tsx), App.tsx passes the unlocked name into the provider, and AppDataContext.tsx:104 destructures it as `activeUser: _activeUser` — deliberately unused. The ONLY per-user state in the entire app is the tutorial flag `tutorial_completed_${userName}` (src/components/Tutorial/Tutorial.tsx:95,278). So Claire logging in sees a pixel-identical app to Scott: same insights, same views, no 'her' anything. Worse, UserManagementPanel.tsx:103 promises 'Each user has their own personalization' — copy that oversells reality and sets a partner expectation the app breaks on first use. The backend is deliberately single-user for Phase 1 (docs/state.md:187 — db-pool ensures exactly one users row; schema is partner-ready), so full partner-mode is correctly parked — but the false Settings copy and the zero-cost seams below (audit actor, fun-money attribution) are actionable now. North-star principle #3 ('Co-op, not shared visibility... neither is reduced to a viewer') is currently inverted: Claire isn't even a viewer with her own lens, she's Scott's session with a different emoji.

verdict.note: Confirmed: AppDataContext.tsx:104 destructures `activeUser: _activeUser` and never uses it; activeUser's only live consumers are the sidebar avatar (AppShell.tsx:156-162) and the tutorial flag `tutorial_completed_${userName}` (Tutorial.tsx:95,278), while UserManagementPanel.tsx:103 verbatim promises "Each user has their own personalization" — the only near-counterexample (per-user streaks in ProgressTracker.tsx:42-53) is dead code that is never imported, and docs/state.md:187 + north-star.md:65 citations are accurate.

confirmed.title: Hardcoded 'Scott'/'Claire' literals silently break fun-money tracking for any other names
confirmed.detail: AppDataContext.tsx:340 and :471 map FunMoney person → spend category via `f.person === 'Scott' ? 'fun_scott' : f.person === 'Claire' ? 'fun_wife' : null`. But the flows that CREATE fun-money entries default to other names: actionStore.ts:445-448 uses 'Person A'/'Person B', DynamicActionForm.tsx:151-153 uses 'Primary'/'Spouse'. Any entry not literally named Scott or Claire never gets monthlySpent auto-synced — it silently stays 0, so the 'remaining' figure shows the full budget forever (an actively wrong number, worst kind for a non-finance partner). Related Scott-creep in the same path: the category enum itself is fun_scott/fun_wife (src/types/budget.ts:4-5); BudgetView.tsx:1416 picks the emoji by name (`fm.person === 'Scott' ? '🎮' : '💅'` — anyone not named Scott gets nail polish); and transactionCategorize.ts:144 auto-routes SeatGeek/StubHub/Ticketmaster to fun_scott, baking 'tickets = Scott's fun' into the shared classifier. The 2026-04-27 handoff claimed 'Scott/Claire hardcodes stripped' — these survived. Fix is small: resolve person→category through the Earner profiles that HouseholdEarners.tsx already collects.

verdict.note: Confirmed: AppDataContext.tsx:340 and :471 contain the exact hardcoded ternary (Scott→fun_scott, Claire→fun_wife, else null) and grep shows it is the only person→category mapping in the codebase; the creation flows really default to 'Person A'/'Person B' (actionStore.ts:446-447) and 'Primary'/'Spouse' (DynamicActionForm.tsx:151-152), so auto-sync of monthlySpent silently no-ops for them, and budgetDefaults.ts:358's comment ("populated by cold-start wizard via Earner profiles") proves the earner-based resolution was intended but never wired (OnboardingView.tsx has zero funMoney references). Only nits: BudgetView emoji line is 1415 not 1416, and a manual monthlySpent edit field exists as a workaround — neither changes the verdict.

unverified.title: Audit log records no actor — 'who changed the budget' is unanswerable with two users
unverified.detail: AuditEntry (src/stores/auditLogStore.ts:17-26) has timestamp/action/entity/details but no user field; auditBudgetEdit (line 114) logs 'Budget edited — 2 edited' with full before/after diffs but not who did it. North-star principle #10 exists precisely to 'catch self-cheating', and with PIN-per-user already shipped, the identity is sitting in App.tsx state at edit time — it's just never threaded into logAuditEvent. This is the single cheapest real multi-user seam in the codebase today: add `actor: activeUser` to entries and the audit log becomes the first surface where Scott and Claire are distinct people. Without it, the moment Claire gets edit access, every budget change is anonymous and the 'blame the budget, not each other' premise loses its receipts.

unverified.title: Fun Money — the app's only per-person surface — is buried inside Edit Budget mode
unverified.detail: The Fun Money card (BudgetView.tsx:1401-1444) renders only inside `{editMode && ...}`. Its own copy says 'This is what stops the money fights' — yet it is invisible during normal daily use: not on the Dashboard, not in the read-mode Budget view. A partner can never glance at 'my fun money: $230 left this month' without entering budget-editing chrome, which is exactly the screen a non-finance partner will never open. The labels compound it: 'Personal Fun (1)' / 'Personal Fun (2)' (ExpenseManager.tsx:19-20, transactionAnalysis.ts:185-186) with guideline copy 'No-judgment spending for the primary earner / secondary earner' (budgetDefaults.ts:335-336) — ordinal, hierarchical, and depersonalized, the opposite of the 'player not viewer' framing. Minimum unlock for a shared daily ritual: two named fun-money meters (real names from Earner profiles) on DashboardView, read-only, next to SavingsScorecard. The data, the spend-sync (once de-hardcoded), and the over-budget insight (insightsEngine.ts:372-396 already says "Claire's fun money over by $X") all exist — only the surfacing is missing.

unverified.title: No per-person attribution anywhere: expenses have no 'who', and earner linkage is collected but never wired
unverified.detail: The Expense interface (src/types/budget.ts:70-88) has no person/earner field — there is no way to record 'who spent this', and the one-click toggle precedent already exists (Personal↔Work in ExpenseManager), so a Scott/Claire/Both toggle would be a natural twin. On the income side the hook EXISTS but is dead: IncomeSource.earnerId (budget.ts:106) is always set to undefined by the detector (incomeDetector.ts:481) and no UI links a source to an earner — so the per-earner profiles HouseholdEarners.tsx painstakingly collects (name, pay shape, cadence, take-home) feed nothing downstream. Concretely: the SavingsScorecard's 'guaranteed base' and the IncomeSources list can't say 'Scott's paycheck' vs 'Claire's paycheck', which matters the moment Claire returns to work (the Sept-2025 single-income cutoff in the data is exactly this scenario in reverse). Smallest wire: an earner dropdown on each IncomeSource row in IncomeSources.tsx, populated from getEarners().

unverified.title: Residual finance jargon a non-finance partner can't parse: OTE, RSU, 'live on base, sweep the rest', 'Surplus / Deficit'
unverified.detail: The vocabulary audit logged in docs/post-phase-1-backlog.md:260-288 partially shipped (HYSA→'Savings' in VariableSurplusCard.tsx:20, 'Stashes (formerly Sinking Funds)' in BudgetView.tsx:1445) but specific leaks remain on partner-facing surfaces: (1) HouseholdEarners.tsx:201 — 'if you'd rather live at OTE pace' — sales-comp jargon, ironically inside the very form used to set up the partner's income; (2) SavingsScorecard.tsx:31 — 'variable/RSU = surplus' on the Dashboard hero scorecard, RSU never expanded; (3) VariableSurplusCard.tsx:175 — header 'Live on base, sweep the rest' assumes the base/sweep mental model before explaining it (the backlog itself flags 'above base' as CFO-grade); (4) BudgetView.tsx:561 'Surplus / Deficit' — accountant register, while the same file's better register already exists at line 902 ('Saved This Month'); (5) income subtype chips 'Base / Variable / Dividend' exposed raw in IncomeSources.tsx:26-33 dropdowns. ('Watermark' and 'reserves' are deliberate vocabulary and read well in context — BudgetView.tsx:877's 'Your monthly watermark — stay under it' is actually a model of the right register.) This is a half-day copy pass, already spec'd in the backlog.

unverified.title: Reserve set-asides are Scott's personal numbers hardcoded in TypeScript — invisible and uneditable for a partner
unverified.detail: RESERVE_ALLOCATIONS (src/utils/budgetLanes.ts:38-42) hardcodes taxes $1,500/mo and travel_personal $1,000/mo, derived per the comment from 'real annual actuals (Scott, 2026-06)'. These numbers surface in the UI ('$1,500/mo reserved · lumpy, not a monthly bust', BudgetView.tsx:612) but cannot be changed anywhere in the app. For the couples lens this matters doubly: 'how much are we putting aside for travel?' is precisely the kind of shared decision both partners should be able to see and adjust together, and travel is the most couple-shaped category in the budget. The file's own header acknowledges the data-vs-code tradeoff (buckets get clobbered by the running app) — but a settings-backed override with these constants as fallback would keep the durability while making the reserve plan a joint, visible decision instead of a code constant only Claude can edit.

unverified.title: GoalTracker — the natural shared-goals surface — is fully built and rendered nowhere
unverified.detail: src/components/Dashboard/GoalTracker.tsx (268 lines) is imported by zero files (verified: only self-references in the repo). It renders exactly the shared-progress experience the couples thesis needs: per-goal progress bars, 'On track' / 'Behind — need $X/mo more' status, projected completion dates, an emergency-fund-as-6-months goal, and an overall '% complete' rollup — the closest thing in the codebase to a 'joint collection book' (the Phase 2 Path B concept in docs/post-phase-1-backlog.md:207). Meanwhile stashes are only visible/editable deep in BudgetView edit mode (BudgetView.tsx:1445+), so shared goals like a vacation fund have no glanceable surface. Wiring GoalTracker into DashboardView with the existing sinkingFunds data is nearly free and is the single highest-leverage 'shared money hobby' move available without opening Phase 2: goals are the one budget object that is inherently 'ours' rather than Scott's. (ProgressTracker, NudgeCenter, and SynthesisDigest are similarly orphaned — worth a deliberate keep-or-delete pass.)
